message.content: Adversaries may exploit the SSH authorized_keys file to maintain persistence on compromised hosts by adding their own public keys, allowing them to log in remotely using the corresponding private key. This technique is prevalent in Linux and macOS environments where key-based SSH authentication is common. Attackers can modify the authorized_keys file directly via scripts or shell commands, or through cloud service APIs and command line interfaces, potentially achieving privilege escalation by adding keys to higher-privileged accounts. Notable groups like Bundlore, Earth Lusca, Skidmap, TeamTNT, and XCSSET have used this method to ensure continued access to compromised systems. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting changes to the authorized_keys file, employing multi-factor authentication, and regularly auditing SSH configurations and key files for unauthorized modifications.
